summary: Hotz reflects on 'the reckoning' he predicted 10 years ago — the displacement of the professional managerial class by machines — and finds it arriving in a darker way than expected. He criticizes AI marketing for maximizing fear while offering little positive vision, and laments that American society lacks the communal fabric needed to navigate this transition well. Drawing on quotes from Dune, Yudkowsky, and Curtis Yarvin, he argues that AI won't fix deep societal problems and that the revolution will be painful, potentially culling 90-99% of people from relevance. Despite his lifelong dreams of this technology, he finds himself unable to be excited about how it's unfolding.
key_points: AI marketing has been counterproductive — oscillating between existential threat and job replacement while charging $20/month for access, creating justified public fear rather than excitement
American society's lack of communal identity makes it uniquely ill-equipped to handle the AI transition, contrasted with places like Hong Kong where collective identity is stronger
The 'reckoning' of the professional managerial class is arriving, but winners are being ungracious about it rather than building bridges
AI won't solve fundamental societal problems — technology alone has never been sufficient without social cohesion
The coming displacement won't be decided by any authority but by 'chips falling where they may,' potentially leaving only 1-10% of people relevant
money_quotes: Here's this machine. In the best case, it takes your job. In the worst case, it wipes out humanity. Pay me $20 a month for a sliver of hope of not falling behind.
I wish it didn't have to be this way. AI could bring about such a golden age.
There's never been a revolution people are less excited for, and they aren't wrong.
I've dreamed about this for my whole life and I'm not even excited about it. Not like this.
Are we going to remember we live in a society? Probably. But after we cull at least 90% of people.
The value of cleaning up homelessness would pay itself back 100 fold.
Our problems in the world won't be fixed by AI.
Like all revolutions, the only way out is through.
key_insight: The AI revolution is arriving not as the golden age technologists dreamed of, but as a joyless reckoning that exposes and deepens society's broken social fabric rather than repairing it.
